templates.name: MSSQL Default Instance (Performance Counters)
templates.comments: Monitors the default Microsoft SQL Server instance (MSSQLSERVER) on Windows via PDH performance counters. Server-wide counter paths are hardcoded to the SQLServer:* prefix used by the default instance. Per-database DCIs use PDH instance discovery against the SQLServer:Databases object, so a DCI is created for every database the instance hosts.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Resource Pool Stats(default)\CPU usage %
dcis.comments: SQL Server CPU usage attributable to the default Resource Governor pool. This is SQL Server's view of CPU usage; host-wide CPU should be tracked separately.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Memory Manager\Target Server Memory (KB)
dcis.comments: Memory SQL Server is trying to consume. Persistent gap between target and total indicates memory pressure or that the engine is still ramping up.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Memory Manager\Memory Grants Pending
dcis.comments: Queries waiting for a memory grant. Sustained non-zero values indicate memory starvation.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Memory Manager\Memory Grants Outstanding
dcis.comments: Queries that have successfully acquired a memory grant and are running.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Buffer Manager\Page life expectancy
dcis.comments: Seconds a data page stays in the buffer pool without reference. Sustained drops indicate memory pressure.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Buffer Manager\Buffer cache hit ratio
dcis.comments: Buffer cache hit ratio is returned by PDH as an integer percentage (0-100). Values consistently below ~90 indicate memory pressure.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Plan Cache(_Total)\Cache Pages
dcis.comments: Total 8 KB pages consumed by all plan cache stores. Multiply by 8 to get KB.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Databases(_Total)\Log Flushes/sec
dcis.description: MSSQL: log flushes per second (all databases)

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Databases(_Total)\Log Bytes Flushed/sec
dcis.description: MSSQL: log bytes flushed per second (all databases)

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Transactions\Version Store Size (KB)
dcis.comments: Total size of the version store in tempdb (used by RCSI/SI, online index, MARS, AFTER triggers). Sustained growth often points to a long-running transaction blocking version cleanup.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:SQL Errors(_Total)\Errors/sec
dcis.description: MSSQL: SQL errors per second (all severities)

dcis.name: \SQLServer:SQL Errors(User Errors)\Errors/sec
dcis.comments: Errors raised by user-issued statements (severity 11-16). Server errors (severity >= 19) are tracked under the _Total counter and other SQL Errors instances.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Access Methods\Forwarded Records/sec
dcis.comments: Reads of forwarded records in heaps. Persistent non-zero values indicate fragmented heaps that should be rebuilt or converted to clustered indexes.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Buffer Manager\Free list stalls/sec
dcis.description: MSSQL: free list stalls per second
dcis.comments: Requests that had to wait for a free buffer page. Sustained non-zero values indicate severe buffer pool pressure.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Wait Statistics(Cumulative wait time (ms) per second)\Lock waits
dcis.description: MSSQL: cumulative wait time per second - Lock waits
dcis.comments: Wait time accumulated per real-time second across all sessions waiting on locks. A value of 1 s/s means one fully-blocked session-equivalent. Compare relative magnitudes across wait-category DCIs to find the dominant bottleneck.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Wait Statistics(Cumulative wait time (ms) per second)\Memory grant queue waits
dcis.description: MSSQL: cumulative wait time per second - Memory grant queue waits
dcis.comments: Sustained pressure here means queries are queuing for memory grants - typically RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE waits. Sign of memory under-provisioning or runaway grant requests.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Wait Statistics(Cumulative wait time (ms) per second)\Network IO waits
dcis.description: MSSQL: cumulative wait time per second - Network IO waits
dcis.comments: ASYNC_NETWORK_IO equivalent - SQL Server is waiting for the client to consume result rows. High values usually mean a slow client, not a server problem.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Wait Statistics(Cumulative wait time (ms) per second)\Page IO latch waits
dcis.description: MSSQL: cumulative wait time per second - Page IO latch waits
dcis.comments: PAGEIOLATCH_* waits - reading/writing data pages from/to disk. The most reliable disk-latency indicator from SQL Server's point of view.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Wait Statistics(Cumulative wait time (ms) per second)\Page latch waits
dcis.description: MSSQL: cumulative wait time per second - Page latch waits
dcis.comments: PAGELATCH_* waits - in-memory contention on data pages, typically tempdb allocation pages (PFS/GAM/SGAM) or hot tables.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Wait Statistics(Cumulative wait time (ms) per second)\Log write waits
dcis.description: MSSQL: cumulative wait time per second - Log write waits
dcis.comments: WRITELOG waits - flushing transaction log records to disk. High values point to slow log storage or log-disk contention.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Wait Statistics(Cumulative wait time (ms) per second)\Log buffer waits
dcis.description: MSSQL: cumulative wait time per second - Log buffer waits
dcis.comments: LOGBUFFER waits - sessions waiting for free space in the log buffer. Often follows sustained Log write waits.

dcis.name: \SQLServer:Databases({instance})\Log Growths
dcis.comments: PDH returns the raw cumulative count of log file autogrowths since instance start; the server converts to per-minute rate via DCM_AVERAGE_PER_MINUTE delta calculation. Each autogrowth is an expensive event - sustained non-zero rates indicate the log isn't sized appropriately.

scripts.name: MssqlDatabases
